21 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Over the past two years, artificial intelligence has arguably become the most talked-about topic in the IT industry. It’s now almost impossible to open a tech news feed without seeing another headline claiming that AI will soon replace software engineers, that yet another startup has raised millions of dollars thanks to AI, or that a major technology company has switched its employees to AI coding agents while reducing the size of its engineering teams.
At the same time, the software engineering job market has been going through one of its most challenging periods in recent years. The number of open positions has declined, entry-level developers are finding it increasingly difficult to land their first job, and even experienced engineers are discovering that recruiters are no longer reaching out as aggressively as they did just a few years ago. Finding a new position can now take months.
This raised an obvious question for me: is artificial intelligence really the primary reason behind these changes, or are we witnessing several independent trends that simply happened to converge at the same time?
Over the past few months, I’ve read dozens of industry reports, engineering blogs, interviews with technology executives, and research papers on the adoption of generative AI in software development. The more I learned, the clearer it became that reality is far more nuanced than the headlines suggesting that AI is about to replace software engineers overnight.
In this article, I’d like to bring those pieces together. We’ll look at how some of the world’s largest technology companies are integrating AI into their engineering workflows, what measurable benefits they’ve reported, what unexpected challenges their development teams have already encountered, and why more and more people are beginning to ask whether we’re witnessing an AI bubble. Finally, I’ll share my own perspective on how these trends could shape the software engineering industry over the next several years.
Before we begin, I’d like to make one thing clear: this article is not presented as absolute truth. Some of the material is based on publicly available data published by large technology companies, some comes from industry research, and the conclusions are ultimately my own interpretation of what’s happening today. It’s entirely possible that a few years from now some of my predictions will prove too optimistic — or too conservative.
But at a time when our industry is evolving almost monthly, I believe it’s worth stepping back, connecting the dots, and trying to see the bigger picture.
How Artificial Intelligence Has Changed Software Development
Just a few years ago, most software engineers viewed AI as an interesting technological breakthrough rather than a practical tool for everyday work. Early versions of GitHub Copilot could autocomplete individual lines of code, while ChatGPT was useful for explaining error messages or generating small scripts. At the time, however, serious discussions about AI replacing software engineers still felt premature.
Everything changed rapidly in 2023 with the release of GPT-4, followed by Claude 3, Gemini, Cursor, Windsurf, and a new generation of AI-powered development tools. For the first time, developers could do far more than generate isolated code snippets. They could have an ongoing conversation with a language model, iteratively refining solutions, fixing bugs, discussing architectural decisions, and even planning the structure of an entire application.
The shift became even more significant with the emergence of AI coding agents. Unlike traditional chatbots, which simply answered questions, these agents could actively interact with a codebase. They were able to analyze existing projects, create new files, execute terminal commands, run test suites, fix compilation errors, and even open pull requests automatically. In many ways, large language models evolved from search assistants into active participants in the software development process.
For many engineers, this fundamentally changed the way they work. Instead of writing every function from scratch, developers increasingly describe the problem in natural language and receive a working implementation that can then be reviewed, refined, and integrated into an existing codebase. As a result, the role of the software engineer is gradually shifting away from writing code toward defining requirements, validating quality, making architectural decisions, and ensuring that generated solutions fit the broader system.
This transformation explains why virtually every major technology company is investing enormous resources into AI. Microsoft continues to expand GitHub Copilot, Google is integrating Gemini across its ecosystem, Amazon is developing its own AI-powered engineering assistants, Meta is embedding generative AI into internal workflows, while OpenAI and Anthropic are effectively competing to become the primary platform powering the next generation of AI-assisted software development.
The business perspective has evolved just as dramatically. In 2023, many organizations were still running pilot projects and cautiously evaluating the potential of generative AI. By 2025 and 2026, however, the conversation had shifted. Executives were no longer asking whether AI should be adopted — they were debating how deeply it should be integrated into engineering workflows and how much additional productivity it could unlock. For many organizations, AI has moved beyond experimentation and become a core part of their long-term engineering strategy.
Perhaps the most interesting development is that major technology companies have begun publishing concrete results from their AI adoption efforts. Instead of making vague claims about an “AI revolution,” they now share measurable engineering metrics: increases in pull request volume, faster product delivery, higher developer productivity, and significant reductions in time spent on repetitive coding tasks.
These real-world examples explain why AI has become one of the defining technologies of the past few years. Before discussing the challenges, the AI bubble, or the future of software engineering, it’s worth examining how the industry’s largest companies are already using AI today. That’s exactly what we’ll explore in the next section.
How the World’s Largest Tech Companies Are Adopting AI
When it comes to integrating AI into software development, every company is taking a slightly different approach. Some focus primarily on improving developer productivity, others are redesigning their internal engineering processes, while a growing number now consider AI proficiency to be a fundamental professional skill.
One of the most striking examples is Shopify. In the spring of 2025, the company’s CEO published an internal memo that quickly became public. It stated that using AI should become a core competency for every employee. More importantly, before requesting additional headcount or opening a new position, managers were expected to demonstrate that the work could not be completed effectively with AI assistance. In other words, Shopify fundamentally changed the way it approaches hiring and resource planning: AI first, hiring second.
Duolingo adopted a similar strategy by announcing its transition toward an AI-first organization. The company explained that future business processes would be designed with AI capabilities in mind, and that effectively using AI would become one of the key skills expected from employees. Although Duolingo later softened some of its initial messaging after receiving public criticism, its overall direction remained unchanged: AI is now considered an integral part of everyday work.
At Microsoft, AI is no longer viewed as a standalone product. GitHub Copilot has become a core component of the company’s engineering ecosystem and is used by millions of developers worldwide. Microsoft executives have repeatedly stated that AI should become as essential to software engineers as modern IDEs or version control systems. Rather than replacing developers, Copilot is positioned as a tool that enables them to work more efficiently.
Amazon has expressed a similar vision. According to the company’s leadership, generative AI is being introduced across nearly every business unit. The expectation is that relatively small engineering teams will eventually be able to deliver work that previously required significantly larger organizations. From Amazon’s perspective, AI is primarily a tool for improving productivity and optimizing operational costs.
Meta has taken a somewhat different approach. Instead of focusing exclusively on code generation, the company emphasizes automating internal engineering workflows. AI is used to review documentation, process repetitive internal requests, assist with parts of the code review process, and automate numerous routine tasks, allowing engineers to concentrate on more complex technical problems.
Perhaps the most interesting example comes from Spotify. Rather than relying solely on third-party AI tools, the company developed its own internal AI engineering platform called Honk. According to publicly available information, the vast majority of Spotify engineers regularly use AI models and coding agents as part of their daily workflow. Since introducing these tools, the number of pull requests created by developers has increased by approximately 76%, while internal AI agents automatically generate and merge thousands of code changes across the company’s repositories. Spotify’s engineering leadership has openly stated that the primary bottleneck is no longer writing code — it’s reviewing, validating, and maintaining the ever-growing volume of generated changes.
The same trend can be observed across the broader industry. Google has reported that a significant portion of newly written code is now generated with AI assistance. Anthropic has stated that Claude is responsible for producing a large share of the company’s internal code, contributing to substantial productivity gains. Meanwhile, monday.com has publicly reported developer productivity improvements of more than 30% following the large-scale adoption of generative AI.
Looking across all these examples, one common pattern becomes clear. At this stage, very few companies openly discuss replacing software engineers with AI. Instead, their primary objective is to increase the productivity of existing teams, automate repetitive work, shorten development cycles, and reduce operational costs.
At first glance, it might seem that AI is solving many of software engineering’s long-standing challenges. However, as development velocity continues to increase, a new set of problems has begun to emerge. The faster organizations adopt AI, the more they encounter unexpected side effects: growing technical debt, increasingly complex codebases, heavier code review workloads, and new maintenance challenges that barely existed just a few years ago.
Ironically, these engineering challenges have become some of the most interesting conversations taking place across the industry today. Before discussing the future of software engineering as a profession, it’s worth understanding the practical problems AI has already introduced into modern software development.
The AI Bubble: An Overhyped Trend or the Next Industrial Revolution?
As AI becomes increasingly embedded in software development, another question has started gaining traction — one that seemed premature just a few years ago:
Are we witnessing the formation of an AI bubble?
Opinions are deeply divided.
Some believe AI represents the most significant technological breakthrough since the rise of the internet. Others argue that the market has begun assigning hundreds of billions of dollars in value to AI companies long before many of them have established sustainable business models.
These concern
